metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `source=backfill_classified` + `confidence=classified` to the fields written on every `caused_by` link in the backfill script, and add an AC asserting these fields are present on written links (satisfying parent Success Criteria last bullet and AC5); also add or reference an AC asserting `is_authoritative('backfill_classified')` returns False (satisfying parent AC3).
evidence: Parent Success Criteria (last bullet): 'every backfilled/classified value is labeled source=backfill_classified and confidence=classified (the single backfill vocabulary — there is no backfill_structural), and is_authoritative() returns False for it so it is never aggregated into an authoritative structural series.'

Parent AC5: 'Backfill is validated: at least one backfilled caused_by/close_class/snapshot value exists carrying source=backfill_classified + confidence=classified, asserted by a test over the backfill output.'
Parent AC3: 'a unit test asserts is_authoritative("backfill_classified") is False and the segregation holds.'
Leaf AC1: 'the script links the single-culprit bug and skips the ambiguous one (assert links == 1)' — no mention of source/confidence fields.
Leaf AC2 and AC3 likewise assert only link count and idempotency, not field content.
ABSENCE: The words 'backfill_classified', 'confidence=classified', and 'is_authoritative' do not appear anywhere in the leaf plan.
findings.finding: The leaf plan is entirely silent on the parent's mandatory backfill-labeling contract: the parent epic's Success Criteria explicitly require every backfilled value to carry `source=backfill_classified` + `confidence=classified`, and parent AC5 requires a test asserting this. The leaf's three ACs assert only link count and idempotency — none require the written `caused_by` link to carry these provenance fields, nor does any AC touch `is_authoritative()` or the segregation property mandated by parent AC3.
findings.location: Leaf: Approach section, Tests section, Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs); Parent: Success Criteria (last bullet), AC3, AC5
scenarios: An implementer who writes caused_by links without source/confidence fields passes all three leaf ACs but violates the parent's mandatory vocabulary — the backfill data would be indistinguishable from authoritative structural data and could be incorrectly aggregated.

Parent AC5 ('Backfill is validated: at least one backfilled caused_by value carrying source=backfill_classified + confidence=classified, asserted by a test') cannot be satisfied by this leaf's tests as written.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'Every `caused_by` link written by the backfill script carries `source="backfill_classified"` and `confidence="classified"` in its event payload, conforming to the single backfill vocabulary.' In the Acceptance Criteria, extend AC3 (or add a fourth AC): 'The written link record carries `source=backfill_classified` and `confidence=classified`, asserted by reading the raw link event from the fixture store after the write-mode run; and `is_authoritative("backfill_classified")` returns False (unit-tested).'

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC1 vs AC3: if AC1 is meant to test dry-run mode (the default), say so explicitly ('run in dry-run mode, assert 0 links written but 1 proposed'); if AC1 is redundant with AC3, remove it.
evidence: AC1: 'the script links the single-culprit bug and skips the ambiguous one (assert links == 1)'
AC3: 'asserts exactly ONE caused_by link is drawn (to the single-culprit's resolved ticket) and the ambiguous bug gets none'
AC1 does not mention --dry-run or --write; AC3 explicitly states 'write mode'
findings.finding: AC1 and AC3 are near-duplicate: both assert exactly ONE caused_by link is drawn from a two-bug fixture (one single-culprit, one ambiguous). AC1 does not specify the invocation mode (dry-run vs write), making it unclear whether it tests a distinct scenario (dry-run preview) or simply restates AC3 (write-mode end-to-end).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 and AC3
scenarios: An implementer writes a single test covering write-mode and believes both ACs are satisfied, inadvertently leaving the dry-run default behavior untested.
Alternatively, an implementer writes two tests that are functionally identical, providing false confidence.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC1 with: 'AC1 (dry-run): invoke the script in default (--dry-run) mode over the two-bug fixture; assert it prints/returns 1 proposed link but writes 0 actual caused_by edges to the store.'

findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria cover only the unit-test fixture, not the actual execution of the backfill script against real/production data. The stated end-state ('seeding source-of-bugs history') is never verified: no AC requires running `python scripts/backfill_caused_by.py --dry-run` against the real tracker store and asserting that N links would be drawn (or were drawn), so a passing test suite does not prove the backfill was ever run or produced any result.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (both ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a third AC such as: '[ ] Running `python scripts/backfill_caused_by.py --dry-run --tracker-dir <real_path>` exits 0 and reports ≥1 candidate `caused_by` link; after a live run (without `--dry-run`), `assert graph.caused_by_count() > 0` or equivalent query confirms links exist in the store.' Attach the dry-run output to the ticket as the completion artifact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a concrete retire path for backfilled links: either tag each written link event with a `source=backfill` metadata field (so a rollback script can identify and remove them) or add a `scripts/rollback_caused_by.py` counterpart that removes links written by the backfill.
evidence: Plan says only 'back-out is removing the additive links' — no rollback command, no tagging of backfilled events, no retire script mentioned
The plan acknowledges the writer bypasses `add_dependency`'s closed-source guard, making backfilled links structurally indistinguishable from going-forward ones unless tagged
No RETIRE/cleanup path is enumerated for the shared link state being created
findings.finding: The retire/rollback path for written links is specified only as 'removing the additive links' with no mechanism, command, or script named. For a one-time backfill that writes directly via `_write_link_event` (bypassing normal `add_dependency` guards), there is no defined way for an operator to identify, enumerate, or remove the backfilled links if the backfill is found to be wrong.
findings.location: Approach section: 'back-out is removing the additive links'
scenarios: An operator discovers the blame resolver produced incorrect culprits for a subset of bugs after the backfill runs in write mode; they have no tool to identify which caused_by edges were backfill-written vs organically produced, making selective rollback impossible.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `backfill_run_id` or `source=backfill` tag to each event written by `_write_link_event` (if the event format supports metadata), and document a rollback command (e.g. `python scripts/backfill_caused_by.py --rollback --run-id <id>`) that deletes only those events.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete rollback mechanism: either reference the `rebar unlink` command with an example invocation, or add a `--undo` mode to the script that re-reads the dry-run output / a written audit log and issues UNLINK events for every `caused_by` link the backfill created.
evidence: Plan quote: 'back-out is removing the additive links' — no mechanism specified.
`src/rebar/_commands/unlink.py` exists and can write UNLINK events, but is not referenced by the plan.
No mention of how to identify backfill-written links vs. pre-existing ones (no provenance tag, no audit log entry).
findings.finding: The back-out path is stated as 'back-out is removing the additive links' but the plan provides no mechanism to actually do this — no UNLINK command invocation, no compensating script, no enumeration of how to identify which links were written by this backfill vs. pre-existing ones. If the backfill writes incorrect links (e.g. due to a stale git blame or resolver bug), there is no described procedure to undo them.
findings.location: Approach section — 'back-out is removing the additive links'
scenarios: Resolver has a bug and writes 50 incorrect `caused_by` links; operator wants to roll back — plan gives no guidance on how to enumerate or remove them.
Partial failure at bug 200 of 500: operator cannot determine which of the first 200 got links without inspecting each ticket directory individually.
findings.suggested_fix: Have the script emit a machine-readable audit log (e.g. a JSON-lines file listing each `(bug_id, culprit_id)` pair written). The rollback procedure is then: for each line in the audit log, run `rebar unlink <bug_id> <culprit_id>` (or a `--undo <audit-log>` flag on the script itself). Reference this in the Approach section so back-out is unambiguous.

findings.finding: The plan's back-out path ('back-out is removing the additive links') does not specify HOW links are removed, does not identify a rollback script or command, and does not assert the post-rollback schema state — specifically whether compiled SNAPSHOT files (which bake LINK events via ticket-compact.sh) also need invalidation after link removal.
findings.location: Approach section — 'back-out is removing the additive links'

findings.finding: The rollback path 'back-out is removing the additive links' is under-specified and potentially ambiguous. LINK events in the store are immutable appended files; 'removing' them could mean (1) deleting the LINK event JSON files from disk, or (2) writing compensating UNLINK events. The plan does not specify the mechanism, does not tag backfill-written LINK events distinctively (e.g., `source=backfill`), and does not describe what the store state looks like after a partial rollback. DATA LOSS RISK: inconsistent partial rollback (e.g. some LINK files deleted, some left) leaves the graph in an incorrect intermediate state that the reducer may misread.
findings.location: Approach section — 'back-out is removing the additive links'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'source': 'backfill_caused_by'` to the LINK event payload written by the script, then provide a `--rollback` mode that issues compensating UNLINK events for all LINK events carrying that source tag. Assert in the plan that after rollback no caused_by edges with source=backfill_caused_by remain active.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit, actionable rollback mechanism (e.g. a `--undo` flag, a compensating deletion helper, or a named file-system operation) so 'back-out is removing the additive links' is implementable, not just stated.
evidence: 'back-out is removing the additive links' — plan states reversibility but names no removal mechanism
The plan describes only `_write_link_event` (a writer) and `_is_active_link` (a reader); no delete/unlink primitive is mentioned anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The back-out plan says 'back-out is removing the additive links' but provides no mechanism for actually removing them — no delete API, no compensating script, no file/store operation is described. The rollback is acknowledged but not actionable.
findings.location: Approach section — 'back-out is removing the additive links'
scenarios: A production backfill writes incorrect caused_by links (e.g. a bad commit→ticket resolution); the operator has no described procedure to remove the erroneous links.

findings.finding: The plan's back-out description ('back-out is removing the additive links') acknowledges reversibility but provides no mechanism — no command, no tooling, no procedure for removing written caused_by links. T4 requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan, not merely an acknowledgement that removal is theoretically possible.
findings.location: Approach section: 'back-out is removing the additive links'

findings.finding: The retire/cleanup path for backfilled `caused_by` links is described only as 'removing the additive links' with no specified mechanism, script, or command — leaving operators with no concrete back-out procedure if the backfill produces incorrect links.
findings.location: Approach section: 'back-out is removing the additive links'
findings.suggested_fix: At write time, include a metadata marker (e.g., `source=backfill_caused_by`) in the link event payload; add a `--undo` flag that deletes all link events carrying that marker, and test the undo path in `test_backfill_caused_by.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an error-handling policy to the Approach section: specify that per-bug blame failures are caught, logged (bug_id + exception), and counted separately, so the script always completes the full bug list and emits a final summary (processed / linked / skipped-ambiguous / errored).
evidence: No retry, try/except, or error-surfacing strategy is mentioned for the blame resolution step
Plan covers idempotency (re-run safety) but not per-bug error isolation
A partially-completed run with some bugs linked and an unhandled exception mid-loop is not discussed
findings.finding: The plan specifies no error handling for git blame failures or file I/O errors during the backfill loop. If `blame.py` raises (e.g., git not available, a file was deleted, a commit no longer exists), the plan is silent on whether the script aborts, skips the bug and continues, or logs and surfaces the failure — leaving partial-run behavior undefined.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'reuse `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`'
scenarios: git blame fails for one bug (deleted file, shallow clone) — unhandled exception aborts the script, leaving partial results with no summary of what was processed
Operator re-runs the script, idempotency skips already-linked bugs correctly, but the original error is never surfaced so the operator doesn't know which bugs were skipped vs failed
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the per-bug resolution in a try/except block; accumulate (bug_id, error) pairs; print a summary table at exit. Exit with a non-zero code if any bugs errored, so CI/operators can detect problems.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error-handling behaviour for the write-mode CLI path: at minimum, define whether blame failures are logged-and-skipped or fatal, and require the script to emit a summary (N linked, M skipped-ambiguous, K errored) on exit so operators can assess completeness.
evidence: AC describes only: single-culprit bug gets a link, ambiguous bug gets none, second run is a no-op — no error case is specified
Approach section: 'Reuse the same conservative single-culprit resolver' — blame can fail (git unavailable, repo history missing for old bugs), but no error handling is described
Plan is a CLI tool with `--dry-run` / `--write` flags — it has a user-facing interaction surface where non-happy-path behaviour matters
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how the script handles error states during write mode — specifically, what happens when blame/git fails for a subset of bugs mid-run. An unhandled exception or silent skip leaves a partial write with no signal to the operator, and re-running cannot distinguish already-written-but-unconfirmed bugs from genuinely skipped ones.
findings.location: Approach section and AC: the plan describes only the happy paths (single-culprit → link, ambiguous → skip) and the idempotency no-op case; error/abort paths are absent.
scenarios: Operator runs the backfill in --write mode over 500 closed bugs; blame fails for bugs 200-250 (e.g. the repo's git history was rebased and those commits no longer exist). The script either crashes at bug 200 (leaving 199 written, 301 unprocessed) or silently skips them. The operator sees no summary of failures and cannot tell how many bugs were actually processed versus errored.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'On blame/git errors for a single bug, log a WARNING and continue (skip that bug); on exit, print a summary line: `Processed N bugs: X linked, Y skipped (ambiguous), Z skipped (error)`. Exit code 0 if Z==0, else 1.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies no error handling for failures during the backfill loop — git blame errors, `_write_link_event` I/O failures, or unexpected exceptions when iterating closed bugs — leaving the script's behavior on partial failure unspecified.
findings.location: Approach section; scripts/backfill_caused_by.py (described behavior)
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each bug's processing in a try/except that logs `logger.warning('bug %s: skipped due to error: %s', bug_id, e)` and increments an error counter, then print a summary at script exit.

findings.finding: The plan's Approach section specifies the write path (call _write_link_event after _is_active_link check) but does not specify the error-handling posture when _write_link_event raises CommandError (documented failure modes: rebase-in-progress guard exit 75, lock timeout). For a batch backfill iterating over N closed bugs, this is load-bearing: an unhandled CommandError on bug K aborts the loop and silently leaves bugs K+1…N unprocessed, while a swallowed error loses the operator's ability to know which writes failed. Neither the Approach section nor the ACs specify fail-fast vs. continue-with-warning, nor whether a summary of failed/skipped/written counts is emitted.
findings.location: ## Approach — write path / error handling
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the per-bug write in a try/except CommandError that logs a warning and increments a failure counter; emit a final summary line (e.g. 'backfill complete: 12 linked, 34 skipped (already active), 2 failed') and exit non-zero if any failures occurred. Since the script is idempotent, fail-continue is the friendlier choice for bulk backfills.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the check-then-act `_is_active_link` → `_write_link_event` pattern with an atomic write (e.g. a file lock around the check+write pair, or a single writer process guarantee) so concurrent runs cannot produce duplicate caused_by edges.
evidence: Plan states: 'because the low-level `rebar.graph._links._write_link_event` writer has NO built-in idempotency'
Plan provides only a check-then-act guard, with no mention of a lock, transaction, or CAS mechanism
Two concurrent runs pass the `_is_active_link` check simultaneously → both call `_write_link_event` → duplicate link events
findings.finding: The idempotency guard is a check-then-act TOCTOU race: the script calls `_is_active_link(...)` and then, only if False, calls `_write_link_event(...)`, but these two operations are not atomic. Two concurrent invocations of the script (e.g. a CI re-run overlapping with a manual run) can both read `_is_active_link` as False before either writes, then both write, producing duplicate `caused_by` link events in the store — exactly the scenario the guard is meant to prevent.
findings.location: Approach section: '…the script guards the write itself — before writing it calls `rebar.graph._links._is_active_link(…)` and SKIPS the bug when an active `caused_by` edge to that culprit already exists.'
scenarios: CI environment: a failed CI job is retried while the first run is still writing — both instances pass the is_active check for the same bug before either commits, resulting in a duplicated caused_by edge in the store.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `_is_active_link` check and `_write_link_event` call in a file-lock (e.g. `filelock.FileLock` on the tracker_dir) so the guard+write is atomic, or document that the script must never be run concurrently and enforce this with a lock file that errors out a second instance.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `--batch-size N` (or `--limit N`) option and/or a progress cursor so the backfill can be run in safe incremental chunks and resumed without re-scanning already-processed bugs.
evidence: Plan says 'iterate closed bugs' with no batch size, sleep/throttle, or chunk limit.
Each bug calls git blame subprocesses via `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` (one per file in file_impact).
No checkpoint cursor is mentioned; the only resumability is the per-bug _is_active_link guard (which still requires re-scanning ALL bugs from the top on a restart).
findings.finding: The backfill iterates all closed bugs in a single unbounded pass with no batching or throttling. Each bug invokes one or more `git blame` subprocesses (one per file in `file_impact`) plus a store write per success. On a large repository with hundreds or thousands of closed bugs this is a long-running, resource-intensive operation with no progress checkpointing — if it crashes midway there is no cursor to resume from, and the operator must re-derive which bugs were already processed by inspecting `_is_active_link` state themselves.
findings.location: Approach section — 'iterate closed bugs'
scenarios: Repository with 5,000 closed bugs: a single run takes 30+ minutes, saturating git subprocess concurrency and potentially overwhelming the store's locked write path.
Script crashes at bug 3,000: restart re-runs git blame for all 3,000 already-processed bugs (links are skipped via _is_active_link, but the blame work is repeated at full cost).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `--batch-size` argument (e.g. default 200) that processes at most N bugs per invocation and records the last-processed bug ID in a sidecar cursor file (e.g. `.rebar/backfill_caused_by_cursor`). A restart reads the cursor and skips already-processed bugs entirely, rather than re-running git blame for them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the architectural decision to bypass `add_dependency` via private `_links` APIs (e.g., add an inline design note in the script module header or a short ADR entry), and acknowledge the coupling risk with a plan to harden it (e.g., expose a `write_link_for_closed_bug()` helper in the public `rebar.graph` surface).
evidence: Plan directly invokes `_write_link_event` and `_is_active_link` (both private, underscore-prefixed) to bypass `add_dependency`
No ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update is listed in ## Files
No acknowledgment of breakage risk if `_links` internals are refactored
findings.finding: The script silently couples to two private internal APIs (`rebar.graph._links._write_link_event` and `rebar.graph._links._is_active_link`) to bypass `add_dependency`'s closed-source guard, but the plan neither acknowledges this coupling risk nor captures the architectural decision in a doc/ADR. Private underscore APIs can be refactored without notice, and future maintainers have no record of why the bypass was necessary.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'because the low-level `rebar.graph._links._write_link_event` writer has NO built-in idempotency … the script guards the write itself'
scenarios: A refactor of `rebar.graph._links` renames or changes the signature of `_write_link_event` — the backfill script silently breaks with no documented reason why it was calling the private API
A future developer sees the private-API call and removes it, not knowing it was the only way to write links for closed bugs
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level docstring in `scripts/backfill_caused_by.py` explaining WHY the private APIs are used (closed-bug guard bypass), and file a follow-up task to expose a `rebar.graph.write_caused_by_link(bug_id, culprit_id, tracker_dir, *, skip_if_exists=True)` function so future scripts do not need to reach into `_links` directly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` as the existing consumer of `blame.derive_caused_by` and confirm the backfill script passes `tracker` as the same type (directory string) used there.
evidence: src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:383: culprit = blame.derive_caused_by(ticket_id, repo_root_str, tracker)

Plan says 'Reuse the same conservative single-culprit resolver as the live auto-derivation' but does not name transition_close.py as the existing caller whose calling convention the backfill script must match.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` is an existing caller of `blame.derive_caused_by` that the plan does not enumerate. Since the backfill script reuses the same function with the same three-argument signature `(bug_id, repo_root, tracker)`, the plan should acknowledge this shared dependency to confirm the function is being called identically (same `tracker` argument semantics) and that no concurrent-use or import-side-effect issues arise.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Reuse the same conservative single-culprit resolver'
scenarios: If the backfill script passes a different type for `tracker` (e.g., a Path object instead of a string, or a tracker object instead of a directory path), `derive_caused_by` may silently return None for every bug — producing zero links with no error.
A reviewer auditing the backfill script has no pointer to the existing call site to confirm argument semantics.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Approach section: 'The backfill script calls `blame.derive_caused_by(bug_id, repo_root_str, tracker_dir)` with the same argument types as `transition_close.py` line 383 — `tracker_dir` is a `str` path to the tracker root.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `culprit == bug_id` (post-canonical-resolution) self-link guard to the backfill script's write logic, mirroring `_apply_caused_by`'s `if not culprit or culprit == ticket_id: return` check in `transition_close.py:384`.
evidence: transition_close.py:384: `if not culprit or culprit == ticket_id: return` — the live path guards against self-links before writing, but this guard is absent from the plan's specified write logic

blame.py:120: `if culprit is None or (resolve_ticket_id(bug_id, tracker) or bug_id) == culprit: return None` — derive_caused_by itself guards the canonical-id case, but an explicit override path in the backfill script could bypass this
The plan's idempotency guard (`_is_active_link`) only checks for an existing edge, not for the self-link case where bug_id == culprit_id after resolution
findings.finding: The plan specifies bypassing `add_dependency` via `_write_link_event` for the closed-source guard, but does not note that the existing prior-art function `_apply_caused_by` (in `transition_close.py`) omits the `culprit == ticket_id` self-link guard only at the `_write_link_event` call site (it checks `if not culprit or culprit == ticket_id: return` before writing). An implementer reading only the plan's Approach section may omit this self-link guard in the backfill script, writing a bug → itself `caused_by` edge when the culprit resolves to the same canonical id — a defect the live path explicitly prevents.
findings.location: Approach section — 'before writing it calls `rebar.graph._links._is_active_link`... and SKIPS the bug when an active caused_by edge to that culprit already exists'
scenarios: A closed bug whose fixing commit references itself (e.g., a squash commit) could produce culprit == bug_id, and the backfill script would write a self-referential caused_by edge not caught by _is_active_link
A ticket alias or canonical resolution maps culprit to the same id as the bug; derive_caused_by would return None but an explicit override path would not
findings.suggested_fix: Before calling `_write_link_event`, resolve both `bug_id` and `culprit` to their canonical ids and skip (with a log/dry-run note) if they are equal, matching the pattern in `_apply_caused_by`.

findings.finding: The plan states '--dry-run by default' in the Approach section, but AC2 asserts 'a second run draws 0 new links' based on _is_active_link finding an existing active link — which can only happen if the first test run actually wrote links (non-dry-run). If the test uses the default dry-run mode, nothing is ever written, _is_active_link always returns False, and every run would 'propose' 1 link, not 0. This is a genuine contradiction between the Approach section and the AC/Tests sections.
findings.location: Approach section ('--dry-run default') vs AC2 ('second run draws 0 new links') vs Tests section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Tests section that the idempotency test explicitly passes `--write` (or `--no-dry-run`) on the first run to commit links, then re-runs to assert 0 new links are written; keep the dry-run default intact but make the test's flag explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the script's output contract in the plan: at minimum, one log line per bug processed (bug_id, outcome: linked/skipped-ambiguous/skipped-exists/error) and a final summary line (e.g., `Processed 42 bugs: 17 linked, 12 skipped (ambiguous), 13 skipped (exists), 0 errors`).
evidence: Only output behavior described is `--dry-run` 'previews before writing'
No mention of per-bug log lines, a final summary count, or any audit trail
Acceptance Criteria assert counts in test fixtures but not in production script output
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability (logging or progress output) for the backfill run. A one-time backfill over potentially many closed bugs with no structured output leaves operators unable to audit what was linked, what was skipped as ambiguous, and what was already present — making it hard to verify correctness or diagnose issues.
findings.location: ## Files — `scripts/backfill_caused_by.py`
scenarios: Operator runs the script in write mode, it completes silently, and there is no way to confirm how many links were drawn without querying the store manually
A dry-run is intended to preview changes, but if output format is unspecified the preview may be unreadable or missing

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the tier label: either rename 'Unit' in the Tests section to 'Scripts-tier' to match the pytestmark, or explain why it is labeled 'Unit' despite living in the scripts tier.
evidence: Tests section: 'Unit (temp store + git): over a fixture of two closed bugs...'
Test tier section: 'The test lives under tests/scripts/ with pytestmark = pytest.mark.scripts (the scripts tier)'
findings.finding: The Tests section labels the test 'Unit (temp store + git)' while the Test tier section places it under tests/scripts/ with pytest.mark.scripts — these are different tier labels for the same artifact, which could confuse CI configuration or tier-based test selection.
findings.location: Tests section ('Unit (temp store + git)') vs Test tier section ('pytestmark = pytest.mark.scripts')
scenarios: A developer reading the Tests section expects this to run in the unit tier (-m unit) and doesn't realize it's only selected by -m scripts, leading to confusion about which CI stage covers it.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'Unit (temp store + git):' in the Tests section to 'Scripts-tier (temp store + git):' to match the pytest.mark.scripts marker.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the inverted dry-run default (--dry-run on by default, --write to activate) is intentional and document why it diverges from backfill_bug_class.py's convention, OR align both scripts to the same CLI convention.
evidence: scripts/backfill_bug_class.py line 137: `parser.add_argument('--dry-run', action='store_true', ...)` — opt-in, NOT default
Plan Approach: '--dry-run by default so a run previews before writing; back-out is removing the additive links'
AC2: 'the test runs the script in WRITE mode (--write, NOT dry-run)'

The sibling script uses standard argparse convention: dry-run off by default; this plan inverts to dry-run on by default with an explicit --write flag to activate writes — a divergent CLI convention in the same scripts/ directory
findings.finding: The plan's --dry-run-as-default / --write opt-in convention diverges from the existing sibling backfill script (backfill_bug_class.py), which uses --dry-run as an opt-in flag with write-mode as the default, creating a config-surface inconsistency across two parallel backfill scripts in the same directory.
findings.location: Approach section ('--dry-run default so a run previews before writing') and AC2 ('--write, NOT dry-run')
scenarios: An operator familiar with backfill_bug_class.py's convention tries the new script without --write and gets a no-op (expected behavior, but surprising given sibling convention).
Future backfill scripts must now choose between two established conventions in the same directory.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the test fixture must seed a `.env-id` file in the tracker directory before invoking the script in write mode, matching the pattern used in `tests/scripts/graph/_helpers.py` (lines 46–52), so the `_seam.append_event` init gate is satisfied.
evidence: src/rebar/_commands/_seam.py line 379: `if not (Path(tracker) / '.env-id').is_file(): raise CommandError('Error: ticket system not initialized.')`
tests/scripts/graph/_helpers.py lines 46-52: every write-capable synthetic-tracker test seeds .env-id before exercising write operations
Plan's Approach section specifies the script 'calls rebar.graph._links._write_link_event' but does not mention .env-id initialization as a prerequisite for the test fixture
ABSENCE: no mention of .env-id seeding in the Tests or Acceptance Criteria sections
findings.finding: The test fixture for the write-mode AC never specifies seeding .env-id, but _write_link_event routes through _seam.append_event which has an unconditional .env-id init gate — it raises CommandError('ticket system not initialized') if the file is absent, causing the 'first pass draws the caused_by link' AC to fail.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — third AC ('the backfill actually executes end-to-end') / Tests section / Approach section (_write_link_event path)
scenarios: Test invokes the script entry point in write mode (--write) over a tmp_path fixture store that lacks .env-id → append_event raises CommandError → no link is written → assert links == 1 fails (or the script swallows the exception and reports 0 links written, still failing the AC)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Tests section, add: 'The fixture must write `tracker / ".env-id"` with a synthetic env-id (e.g. `"00000000-0000-4000-8000-000000000001"`) before calling the script entry point in write mode, mirroring the pattern in `tests/scripts/graph/_helpers.py`.' Also add a git repo stub (bare or real) since `_seam.append_event → write_and_push` commits to git — or confirm the test mocks `_write_link_event` below the seam.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
